prompt: Which of the following is **not** a feature of a **fat tree** topology in a data center network architecture?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It has multiple connections to the core

solution: A **fat-tree** is a *hierarchical* topology built on the classic **three-tier** model (edge → aggregation → core, option D) with **multiple equal-cost paths up to the core** (option B) to provide full bisection bandwidth. It is **not** *recursively organised* — recursion is the hallmark of server-centric topologies like DCell/BCube. Therefore the feature that does **not** belong to a fat tree is **C** (recursive organization).

prompt: In the context of SSDs, one of the following activities is **NOT** managed by the **Flash Translation Layer**:
solution: The **Flash Translation Layer (FTL)** of an SSD hides the peculiarities of NAND flash from the host. Its responsibilities include **address translation** (mapping logical → physical pages, option B), **data allocation / placement** with **wear-leveling** (option D), and **garbage collection** (reclaiming invalid pages by erasing blocks, option C). **Defragmentation** is **not** an FTL task — it is a (host-side) HDD concept and is actually counter-productive on SSDs (which have no seek penalty and have limited write endurance). Hence the activity **not** managed by the FTL is **A** (Defragmentation).

choices.label: A
choices.text: Provides the interface to system ISA and user ISA

choices.label: B
choices.text: Provides the interface to system ABI and the user ISA

choices.label: C
choices.text: Provides the interface to system ABI and the user ABI

choices.label: D
choices.text: Provides the interface to system calls and user ABI

solution: A **System Virtual Machine** virtualizes a complete machine and is implemented at the **ISA (Instruction Set Architecture)** level: it replicates the full hardware interface so that an entire guest OS can run on it. It therefore exposes both the **system-level ISA** (privileged instructions) and the **user-level ISA** (user instructions). A *Process VM*, by contrast, works at the **ABI** level (system calls + user instructions) to run a single application. Hence the System VM provides the interface to **system ISA and user ISA** — answer **A**.

prompt: Consider an array of $N$ disks. Select the right order of the RAID levels in terms of **effective data storage in ascending order** (i.e. from the lower to the higher data storage availability).
solution:
Effective (usable) data capacity per RAID level for $N$ disks:
- **RAID 1+0** (mirroring + striping): only **half** the disks store unique data → usable $=N/2$ (lowest).
- **RAID 6** (double distributed parity): usable $=N-2$ disks.
- **RAID 5** (single distributed parity): usable $=N-1$ disks (highest of the three).

For a typical array ($N>4$): $\dfrac{N}{2} < N-2 < N-1$, so ascending usable capacity is **RAID 1+0 → RAID 6 → RAID 5**, which is answer **A**.

prompt: Warehouse-scale computers belong to a single organization, use a relatively homogeneous hardware and system software platform, and share a common systems management layer.

subStatements.statement: Warehouse-scale computers belong to a single organization, use a relatively homogeneous hardware and system software platform, and share a common systems management layer.
subStatements.motivation: True. These are exactly the defining traits of a **Warehouse-Scale Computer** as introduced in the course: it is owned/run by a **single organization**, built from **relatively homogeneous** hardware and a common system-software stack, and managed through a **common systems-management layer** — which is what lets thousands of servers be operated as one computer. (On the answer sheet the correct box was **B = True**.)

prompt: All faults occurring in a system cause a failure of the system.

subStatements.statement: All faults occurring in a system cause a failure of the system.
subStatements.motivation: False. In the **fault → error → failure** chain a fault only *potentially* leads to a failure. A fault may remain **dormant** (never activated) or be **masked/tolerated** by redundancy and fault-tolerance mechanisms, so it never propagates to an error nor to a service failure. Hence not every fault causes a failure. (On the answer sheet the correct box was **A = False**.)

prompt: In the context of SSDs, Pages are the smallest unit that can be erased.

subStatements.statement: In the context of SSDs, Pages are the smallest unit that can be erased.
subStatements.motivation: False. In NAND flash, the smallest unit that can be **read or written (programmed)** is a **page**, but the smallest unit that can be **erased** is a whole **block** (many pages). This asymmetry (write at page granularity, erase at block granularity) is precisely what forces garbage collection and wear-leveling. So pages are not the smallest erasable unit. (On the answer sheet the correct box was **B = False**.)

prompt: Graphical Processing Units support data-parallel computation.

subStatements.statement: Graphical Processing Units support data-parallel computation.
subStatements.motivation: True. **GPUs** are massively parallel processors built from thousands of simple cores executing the **SIMD/SIMT** (data-parallel) model: the same operation is applied simultaneously to many data elements. This is exactly why they excel at graphics, matrix/tensor math and ML workloads. (On the answer sheet the correct box was **B = True**.)

prompt: A deep neural-network cannot be trained on multiple Graphical Processing Units.

subStatements.statement: A deep neural-network cannot be trained on multiple Graphical Processing Units.
subStatements.motivation: False. Deep neural networks are routinely trained across **multiple GPUs** (and even multiple servers) using **data-parallel** and **model-parallel** strategies, with high-bandwidth interconnects such as NVLink linking the GPUs. Multi-GPU training is in fact the standard way to scale large DNNs. (On the answer sheet the correct box was **B = False**.)

prompt: You are in charge to design a computer system to do a long and extremely critical computation that takes **4 days** to run. The system is composed by **500 servers** and the computation is executed in parallel on all of them. **All the servers should be up and running**, otherwise the computation fails. Considering that we want to achieve a **reliability of 0.9 at $T=4$ days**, what should be the **MTTF** for a server to consider during the system procurement/acquisition phase?
solution:
Since the computation fails unless **every** server stays up for the whole run, from a reliability viewpoint the 500 servers are in **series**:
$$R_{sys}(T)=\big(R_{server}(T)\big)^{500}\ge 0.9$$

**Required per-server reliability:**
$$R_{server}(4)=0.9^{1/500}=e^{(\ln 0.9)/500}=e^{-0.000210721}=0.999789301$$

**Convert to MTTF** (exponential model $R=e^{-T/MTTF}$):
$$MTTF=\frac{-T}{\ln R_{server}}=\frac{-4}{\ln(0.999789301)}=\frac{-4}{-0.000210743}=18982.4\,\text{days}$$

So each server must have a reliability at least this high, i.e. an $MTTF$ **greater than** about $1.90\times10^{4}$ days.

**FINAL RESULT:** $MTTF > \mathbf{18982\ days}\ (\approx 52\ \text{years})$.

prompt: The analysis of the failure behavior of a three-component system (A, B and C) reveals that the system is **down when both B and C are down, or A is down**. The three components have the following characteristics: $MTTF_A=80\,\text{days}$, $MTTR_A=1\,\text{day}$, $MTTF_B=12\,\text{days}$, $MTTR_B=12\,\text{hour}$, $MTTF_C=120\,\text{days}$, $MTTR_C=2\,\text{days}$. What is the **average availability** of the system?
solution:
**Reliability structure.** The system is down if "(B AND C down) OR (A down)"; equivalently it is **up** iff *A is up* **AND** *(B is up OR C is up)*. So **A is in series** with the **parallel block (B ∥ C)**.

**Component availabilities** $A=\dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF+MTTR}$ (with $MTTR_B=12\,\text{h}=0.5\,\text{day}$):
$$A_A=\frac{80}{80+1}=0.987654,\quad A_B=\frac{12}{12+0.5}=0.960000,\quad A_C=\frac{120}{120+2}=0.983607$$

**Parallel block (B ∥ C):**
$$A_{BC}=1-(1-A_B)(1-A_C)=1-(0.040000)(0.016393)=1-0.000656=0.999344$$

**System (A in series with B∥C):**
$$A_{sys}=A_A\cdot A_{BC}=0.987654\times0.999344=0.986806$$

**FINAL RESULT:** $A_{sys}\approx\mathbf{0.9870\ (98.70\%)}$.

prompt: Consider an HDD with **4 KB** block size, a rotation speed of **10000 RPM**, **0.5 ms** transfer time of 1 block, and a negligible controller overhead. Knowing that the average transfer time for a file of **800 KB** is **880 ms**, and the average **locality is 60%**, what is the **average seek time** of the disk?
solution:
The file has $\dfrac{800\,\text{KB}}{4\,\text{KB}}=200$ blocks. Per block:
$$T_{block}=(1-L)\,(T_{seek}+T_{rot})+T_{transfer},\qquad T_{file}=N_{blocks}\cdot T_{block}.$$

**Rotational latency** (half a revolution at 10000 RPM):
$$T_{rot}=\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{60}{10000}\,\text{s}=\frac{1}{2}\cdot 6\,\text{ms}=3\,\text{ms}$$

**Solve for $T_{seek}$** with $T_{file}=880$ ms, $N_{blocks}=200$, $L=0.60$, $T_{transfer}=0.5$ ms:
$$\frac{880}{200}=4.4=(0.4)(T_{seek}+3)+0.5$$
$$4.4-0.5=3.9=0.4\,(T_{seek}+3)\;\Rightarrow\;T_{seek}+3=9.75\;\Rightarrow\;T_{seek}=6.75\,\text{ms}$$

**FINAL RESULT:** average seek time $=\mathbf{6.75\ ms}$.

prompt: A **RAID 4** system uses **eight 2 TB disks** to store data and the required parity bits. Each disk has a Sequential Access Speed (throughput) of **50 MB/s** and a Random Access Speed (throughput) of **5 MB/s**. What is the expected throughput of the RAID 4 considering a **random read** pattern?
solution:
In **RAID 4** parity is concentrated on **one dedicated parity disk**; the other disks hold data. With 8 disks total there are $8-1=7$ **data disks**.

For a **read** workload the parity disk is **not** involved, so all data disks can serve reads **in parallel**. For a **random** read pattern each data disk delivers its **random** access throughput (5 MB/s), and these add up across the data disks:
$$X_{random\;read}=(N-1)\times \text{Random Access Speed}=7\times 5\,\text{MB/s}=35\,\text{MB/s}$$

**FINAL RESULT:** expected RAID 4 random-read throughput $=\mathbf{35\ MB/s}$.

prompt: A company wants to evaluate the performance of its services. The system comprises two servers S1 and S2, initially modeled as an **open queueing network**. During **60-minute** monitoring the following measurements were obtained: System Throughput $X_{Sys}=1.2\,\text{req/s}$; Busy time $B_{S1}=450\,\text{s}$; Throughput $X_{S1}=0.6\,\text{req/s}$; Busy time $B_{S2}=900\,\text{s}$; Throughput $X_{S2}=2.4\,\text{req/s}$. What is the **service demand** for S1 and S2 ($D_{S1}$ and $D_{S2}$)?
solution:
The **service demand** at a resource is the total service time it requires per *system* request. By the Service-Demand Law it equals the resource utilization divided by the system throughput, i.e. the busy time divided by the number of **system** completions:
$$D_k=\frac{U_k}{X_{Sys}}=\frac{B_k/T}{X_{Sys}}=\frac{B_k}{X_{Sys}\cdot T}=\frac{B_k}{C_{Sys}}$$

**Total system completions** over $T=60\,\text{min}=3600\,\text{s}$:
$$C_{Sys}=X_{Sys}\cdot T=1.2\times 3600=4320\ \text{requests}$$

**Service demands:**
$$D_{S1}=\frac{B_{S1}}{C_{Sys}}=\frac{450}{4320}=0.104\,\text{s},\qquad D_{S2}=\frac{B_{S2}}{C_{Sys}}=\frac{900}{4320}=0.208\,\text{s}$$

**FINAL RESULT:** $D_{S1}\approx\mathbf{0.104\ s}$ and $D_{S2}\approx\mathbf{0.208\ s}$.

prompt: Considering the system described in the previous question, what is the **number of visits** for S1 and S2 ($V_{S1}$ and $V_{S2}$)?
solution:
By the **Forced Flow Law**, the visit ratio of a resource is its throughput divided by the system throughput:
$$V_k=\frac{X_k}{X_{Sys}}$$

**Substitute** $X_{Sys}=1.2\,\text{req/s}$:
$$V_{S1}=\frac{0.6}{1.2}=0.5,\qquad V_{S2}=\frac{2.4}{1.2}=2$$

(Consistency check with the Service Demand Law $D_k=V_k\cdot S_k$: $S_{S1}=D_{S1}/V_{S1}=0.104/0.5=0.208\,\text{s}$, $S_{S2}=0.208/2=0.104\,\text{s}$ — both plausible.)

**FINAL RESULT:** $V_{S1}=\mathbf{0.5}$ and $V_{S2}=\mathbf{2}$.

prompt: Considering now the same system as a **closed model** with a think time $Z=6\,\text{s}$, and the same values for the demands $D_{S1}$ and $D_{S2}$ calculated for the open model. In the context of the **asymptotic bounds**, what is the value of $N^*$ after which the bound saturates (remains constant)?
solution:
In closed-model **asymptotic bounds**, the throughput bound switches from the *light-load* line to the *saturation* line at the **saturation point**:
$$N^*=\frac{D+Z}{D_{max}}$$
where $D=\sum_k D_k$ is the total service demand and $D_{max}=\max_k D_k$ is the demand of the **bottleneck** resource.

**Values** (from the previous questions): $D_{S1}=0.104\,\text{s}$, $D_{S2}=0.208\,\text{s}$, so
$$D=D_{S1}+D_{S2}=0.3125\,\text{s},\qquad D_{max}=D_{S2}=0.208\overline{3}\,\text{s},\qquad Z=6\,\text{s}$$

$$N^*=\frac{0.3125+6}{0.208\overline{3}}=\frac{6.3125}{0.208\overline{3}}=30.3$$

**FINAL RESULT:** $N^*\approx\mathbf{30.3}$ (beyond about 30 users the throughput bound saturates at $1/D_{max}$).

prompt: Could you briefly explain the reason why in recent years we also saw a growing interest in the concept of **fog/edge computing**?
solution:
**Fog/edge computing** pushes part of the computation, storage and networking **away from the centralized cloud datacenter and closer to where data is produced and consumed** (sensors, devices, gateways, base stations). Interest in it has grown for several converging reasons:

- **Latency.** Many modern applications (autonomous driving, industrial control, AR/VR, real-time analytics) need **very low, predictable response times**. Sending every request to a distant cloud adds round-trip delay; processing at the edge meets tight latency/real-time requirements.
- **Explosion of IoT data / bandwidth.** The number of connected **IoT devices** has exploded, generating huge volumes of data. Sending all of it to the cloud would **saturate network bandwidth** and is costly; filtering/aggregating/processing data locally drastically reduces the traffic that must travel to the core.
- **Scalability.** Offloading work to many distributed edge nodes relieves pressure on the centralized datacenter and lets the system scale with the number of devices.
- **Privacy, security and data sovereignty.** Keeping **sensitive data local** (processed at the edge instead of shipped to a third-party cloud) helps satisfy privacy regulations and reduces exposure.
- **Reliability / availability and intermittent connectivity.** Edge nodes can keep operating and provide service even when the **connection to the cloud is slow or temporarily unavailable**, improving robustness.
- **Energy / cost.** Reducing long-haul data transfer and offloading from constrained end-devices can lower communication energy and operating cost.

In short, the **continuum** from cloud to edge emerged because the purely centralized cloud model could not satisfy the **latency, bandwidth, scalability, privacy and reliability** demands of the rapidly growing IoT / real-time workloads, so computation is increasingly placed near the data source while the cloud still handles heavy, non-latency-critical processing.
